g.data-narration: Environment variables are dynamic name-value pairs that configure the operating system for processes.
Environment variables store configuration as name-value pairs that programs can read at runtime.
Environment variables control system configuration, shell behavior, development settings, and automation.
Environment variables exist in process memory as name-value pairs the OS maintains for each running program.
g.text: name-value pairs in a special memory area
maintained by the OS for each process.

g.data-narration: Shell variables stay local to the current shell, while exported variables become environment variables inherited by child processes.
PATH lists directories to search for commands, HOME is your home directory, SHELL is your default login shell.
PWD shows current directory, TERM defines terminal type, LANG sets language locale, DISPLAY points to X server.
Use env or printenv to view all variables, echo $VAR to see specific values, and export to set new ones.
The export command makes variables available to child processes. Use unset to remove a variable entirely.
PATH contains colon-separated directories searched left-to-right when you type a command. Add your binaries to PATH for easy execution.
g.text: When you type a command, the shell searches directories in
PATH order (left to right) and executes the first match found.

g.data-narration: When modifying PATH, always append with $PATH to preserve existing directories, never replace the entire variable.
Environment variables persist through configuration files like /etc/profile, ~/.bashrc, and ~/.bash_profile.
Bash reads /etc/profile first, then ~/.bash_profile which typically sources ~/.bashrc for interactive shells.
Use env to list all, export to set, unset to remove, and echo to display environment variables in Linux.
